summary: I was born in Harlan County, Kentucky, a coal-mining area in eastern Kentucky. My father worked in the coal-mining business there, first as an accountant and later as a mine manager. I have vivid memories of the Harlan County strikes when I was about ten, a time marked by bloodshed and fear. The area was very poor during the 1930s, and my family was generally sympathetic to the labor struggle, despite some political differences within the family.

John Henry Blair was my uncle on my mother's side, and he was a county official involved in politics during the unionization of the mines in Harlan County. Unfortunately, he was recorded as not very sympathetic to labor, and the labor unions even wrote a song about him. Despite his political stance, my family generally supported the miners' labor struggles during the strikes.

The song 'Which Side Are You On?' was written by the labor unions during the Harlan County strikes, a period I remember vividly from my childhood. It was a tough time with a lot of bloodshed and fear in the coal-mining community. The song symbolized the division and conflict between labor and management, and it was directly connected to the struggles my family experienced and witnessed.

The Presbyterian Church played a significant role in my education by supporting the boarding school I attended, which was one of their mission schools. This school, Stuart Robinson, was near where my father worked and was largely funded by the church, making it possible for me to attend. The church's involvement helped me access better education than what was available publicly in my rural area.

I attended Stuart Robinson School, a Presbyterian mission boarding school, starting when I was twelve because it was more convenient and offered better education than local public schools. The school had high academic standards, excellent teachers from across the South, and a good library. It was a reassuring and formative experience that confirmed many of my interests and prepared me well for college.

I went to Berea College in the late 1930s and early 1940s, a place known for its commitment to equality and opportunity for all, including men and women and different races. Berea was founded after the Civil War to educate both black and white students and maintained that legacy during my time there. I worked various jobs on campus, from washing dishes to assisting the economics department, and I have remained connected as a trustee, supporting the college financially and in fundraising.

I was asked if I attended Berea College at the same time as Harriet, the novelist, but I did not recognize the name. Therefore, I do not have any memories or connections with Harriet during my time at Berea.

I went to Duke University for graduate school largely because my major professor at Berea, Rector Hardin, had earned his Ph.D. there and encouraged me to continue my studies. At Duke, I worked closely with professors Joseph Spengler and Frank Deviyver, who were influential in my academic development. The wartime period allowed me to teach early due to the shortage of men, which was an important opportunity for me as a woman in the early 1940s.

Rector Hardin was my major professor at Berea College and had earned his Ph.D. at Duke University, which influenced my decision to pursue graduate studies there. He had studied under some of the same scholars I later worked with at Duke. His guidance was instrumental in shaping my academic path and encouraging me to continue my education.

Joseph Spengler was a brilliant and inspiring teacher at Duke University whom I respected deeply. Although I was initially just his student, our relationship grew after I joined the faculty, and we eventually collaborated on writing. While I never considered him a mentor in the traditional sense, he was an important inspirational figure in my academic career.

Frank Deviyver was a true mentor to me during my time at Duke University. I did my dissertation under his supervision and helped him revise one of his books. He was extraordinarily kind, thoughtful, and took a keen interest in his students, including me, and continued to be supportive when I rejoined the faculty.

I consider myself part of the women's movement, reflecting on the challenges and opportunities women faced in academia. During World War II, while many men were in military service, I was able to continue my education and teach earlier than usual, which was a unique opportunity for women at the time. However, I also recognized that after the war, it was harder for women to get teaching jobs compared to men, highlighting ongoing sex discrimination.
